name: Adelia
designer: Fredrik Gruber, Göran Söderström, Léo Guibert
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a flare serif, the design draws on Nordic classicism of the early 1900s with organic, romantic characteristics that bridge transitional and humanist serif traditions.
summary: A flare serif drawing on Nordic arts and classicism of the early 1900s, loaded with quirky alternates and ligatures across two weights.
description: Designed by Fredrik Gruber, Göran Söderström and Léo Guibert at Letters from Sweden, Adelia takes its cues from architecture, painting and books of Nordic classicism, with a particular debt to Walter Käch's typographic poster for a 1950 Contemporary Swiss Arts exhibition. Two weights, Light and Regular, carry an extensive character set with OpenType alternates across eleven stylistic sets, discretionary ligatures and oldstyle numerals. Vietnamese support was added in 2026, broadening the family's Latin coverage considerably.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Adelia as a flare serif influenced by Nordic arts and culture of the early 1900s, paying homage to the final decades of classicism. Inspiration was drawn from architecture, books and paintings, with Walter Käch's typographic poster for the Contemporary Swiss Arts exhibition at Nationalmuseum in 1950 proving a key reference. The family is characterised as elegant and robust, powered with unusual and quirky alternates and ligatures.

name: Bazaar
classification_notes: A gestural, upright script inspired by graffiti and street writing, with variable letterform heights and tight spacing that produce a wild, rhythmic texture. The foundry describes it explicitly as a script typeface, and its irregular stroke paths and baseline behaviour confirm this classification.
summary: A five-weight gestural script by Laurette Colmard, drawn from graffiti and street writing with deliberately unstable letterforms that balance around a centre line rather than the baseline.
description: Letterforms vary in height and lean into disorder rather than conventional harmony, producing a fierce rhythm particularly visible in all-caps settings. The family spans five weights from Light to Black, and includes 38 contextual alternates, 6 ligatures, a set of arrows, icons and broad Latin Extended language support. It is aimed squarely at display and campaign work where visual energy and rule-breaking character are the point.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Bazaar as a gestural and spirited script typeface with letterforms spaced extremely tightly, varying in height and balancing around a centre line rather than sitting on the baseline. Uppercase and lowercase follow peculiar stroke paths inspired by graffiti and street writing, contributing to a wild and fierce rhythm, especially in all-caps. They call it an ode to disorder and a tribute to those who challenge the rules, noting that drawing it required questioning the common principles of balance and harmony that define a well-made typeface.

name: Blåkulla
classification_notes: An all-caps, single-style stencil display face with letterforms derived from architectural observation; the construction method and decorative intent place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-style all-caps stencil display face by My Longley, its letterforms assembled from the curtain patterns observed in the blue-façaded residential blocks of Hagalund, Stockholm.
description: Designed by My Longley as her degree work at Konstfack, the typeface distils arc segments and serif shapes directly from the varied curtain arrangements seen in Hagalund's rectangular windows. The result is a stencil display face where each letter carries an architectural logic, the open and closed curtain sections producing a formal system that feels both found and invented. A single weight covers a broad Latin character set including extended European accents, making it practically usable despite its highly particular origin.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Blåkulla as a single-style, all-caps display face designed by My Longley while living in Hagalund, just outside Stockholm. The typeface takes its name from the neighbourhood's blue residential buildings, and its letterforms were assembled from the patterns of curtains—some closed, some open, some half open—visible in the grid of rectangular windows. Longley began the project as her candidate work at Konstfack, and the foundry describe the result as a link between typography and architecture, with echoes of unknown lives lived behind those countless curtains.

name: Capitaine
classification_notes: A chunky, rounded slab serif with calligraphic italics, reviving the early 20th-century Adtype Series. The rounded, slightly irregular forms and expressive italics place it firmly in the display slab tradition.
summary: A revival of William F. Capitain's Adtype Series from 1912, redrawn by Göran Söderström as a chunky, good-humoured display slab with calligraphic italics.
description: Based on the Adtype Series as published in the American Specimen Book of Type Styles (American Type Founders, 1912), Capitaine preserves the original's rounded, slightly irregular forms while functioning as a contemporary type family. The roman cuts carry a mellow confidence, while the italics are notably energetic and calligraphic, offering a lively contrast to the upright styles. Three weights from Regular to Black give it range across display, editorial and branding contexts where warmth matters more than precision.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Capitaine as a good-humoured, chunky slab serif based on the Adtype Series, found in the American Type Founders' 1912 specimen book. Named after its original designer William F. Capitain, the revival was drawn by Göran Söderström with the aim of preserving the quirky, carefree personality of the source material, including its rounded, slightly irregular features and surprisingly calligraphic italics, as a counterpoint to sharp, regimented display serifs.

name: Eklips
classification_notes: Eklips is a display typeface built around a solar eclipse motif, where intersecting duplicate outlines create ambiguous, stencil-like letterforms with implied three-dimensional volume. Its purely conceptual premise and decorative construction place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A conceptual display typeface by Göran Söderström in which intersecting duplicate outlines evoke the halo of a solar eclipse, producing letterforms that appear both stencilled and ambiguously three-dimensional.
description: Each letter in Eklips is formed by overlapping duplicate outlines that mimic the moment just before totality in a solar eclipse, leaving the reader uncertain whether the forms are lit from left or right. The effect sits somewhere between a classic shadowed face and a stencil, generating a playful perceptual tension that rewards extended reading. Eight weights and scores of stylistic alternates give designers fine control over how far to push that ambiguity.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Eklips as a conceptual typeface inspired by Homo Ludens and the appearance of a solar eclipse just before the moon covers the sun. Intersecting duplicate outlines create character shapes reminiscent of a stencil, with letters suggesting ambiguous three-dimensional volumes that leave the viewer questioning the direction of the light source. The family ships in eight weights with stylistic alternates and oldstyle numerals to let you tune the effect to your taste.

name: Eksell Display
designer: Olle Eksell, Göran Söderström, Krista Radoeva, George Triantafyllakos
classification_notes: A quirky, hand-drawn display typeface with geometric tendencies but irregular, graphic-designer sensibility. Classified as display due to its optical size variants and distinctly decorative character unsuited to text use.
summary: A digitisation of Olle Eksell's 1962 hand-drawn lettering, released as a four-style display family covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
description: Originally sketched in 1962 in a five-square-metre Stockholm studio by the Swedish graphic designer Olle Eksell, the typeface was carefully digitised by Letters from Sweden and released in 2015. The family comprises four optical styles including a stencil cut, and the character set spans Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek. Its irregular, graphic-designer-drawn quality is a feature rather than a flaw, giving it a mid-century warmth that distinguishes it from more polished revivals.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Eksell Display as a unique typeface drawn in 1962 by legendary Swedish designer Olle Eksell, who worked across a broad range of disciplines and completed only two typefaces in his lifetime. The letters were sketched in his tiny Stockholm studio six years after he created the iconic Mazetti eyes. Letters from Sweden digitised the originals and extended them into a family of four optical styles, including a stencil version, with Cyrillic added by Krista Radoeva and Greek by George Triantafyllakos.

name: Eksell Sans
classification_notes: Constructed from straight lines and circle arcs with no weight variation, Eksell Sans is a display typeface in the geometric tradition of 1970s novelty faces, closer to avant-garde lettering than any conventional text category.
summary: A single-weight display face built from straight lines and circular arcs, revived from Olle Eksell's 1973 original alphabet by Göran Söderström in 2021.
description: Originally drawn in 1973 by Olle Eksell, the celebrated Swedish designer and illustrator, this alphabet sat unpublished until Göran Söderström digitised it for Letters from Sweden in 2021. Its letterforms are constructed entirely from straight strokes and circle arcs, placing it in the company of Bob Newman's Zipper and Aldo Novarese's Sintex, though Eksell's version reads as more idiosyncratic and typographically self-aware than either. The result is a face that feels simultaneously retro and unplaceable, suited to editorial, music and cultural contexts where outright strangeness is an asset.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Eksell Sans as the only other alphabet by Olle Eksell (1918–2007), drawn in 1973 as a personal exercise to prove he could master typographic craft. Constructed from straight lines and circle arcs, they note parallels with Zipper and Sintex while emphasising that Eksell's design takes those concepts into entirely new territory. The font was released in 2021 with a single Regular weight and OpenType stylistic sets.

name: Ferry
classification_notes: A single-weight black display typeface drawn from the sturdy painted lettering found on early 20th-century Stockholm steam ferries. The bold, condensed letterforms with distinctive details like the K place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single black-weight display face drawn from lettering found on three early 20th-century Stockholm steam ferries, released as Erik Moberg's first commercial typeface.
description: Derived from nine letters spotted on the 'Stockholms Ström' ferry boats built between 1894 and 1907, the design captures a sturdy, vernacular quality with notably characterful letterforms. Available in one weight only, it covers Latin, Cyrillic and Greek scripts, making it broadly useful for headlines and short display text across multiple languages. Its origins in unattributed painted signage give it an authentic historical character that separates it from generic heavy display faces.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Ferry as inspired by the lettering on three historic Stockholm steam ferries, built between 1894 and 1907 and now used as charter boats in the Stockholm archipelago. Unable to identify the original letterer, designer Erik Moberg built the typeface from those nine source letters. It is his first commercial typeface, released in 2014 in a single black weight with Latin, Greek and Cyrillic support.

name: Funkis
classification_notes: A capitals-first display typeface drawn for bold lettering and signage, rooted in functionalist architectural lettering. The ABC variants introduce geometric, humanist and architectural treatments that align closely with the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A display typeface rooted in Sigurd Lewerentz's lettering for the 1930 Stockholm Exhibition, later expanded into a 120-font variable system with three geometric variants.
description: Originally drawn in 2016 for a collaborative art project, the face takes direct inspiration from the lettering Sigurd Lewerentz produced for the 1930 Stockholm Exhibition, which marked the arrival of functionalism in Scandinavia. The Funkis ABC expansion added a full lowercase and introduced three design variants: A with strict circular geometry and closed apertures, B with softer curves and open forms, and C with straight horizontal transitions recalling architectural signage. A variable font release covers all 120 static styles, with axes controlling weight, sharpness, terminal style, dot shape and ink traps.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Funkis as a display typeface drawn for bold lettering and signage, co-designed with Fredrik Gruber and Göran Söderström. The digits reference characteristic number signs found on functionalist buildings, and the letterforms draw directly from Lewerentz's poster lettering for the 1930 Stockholm Exhibition. The Funkis ABC expansion introduced a customisable system of geometric, soft and architectural variants, now available as a variable font encompassing 120 styles.

name: Gothia Sans
classification_notes: Gothia Sans draws on Dutch Renaissance type models by Van den Keere and Van Dijck, blending their supple elegance with Scandinavian crispness — a humanist sans with clear historical serif roots and a refined editorial bearing.
summary: A humanist sans designed for Göteborgs-Posten by Göran Söderström, drawing on Dutch Renaissance type traditions to serve both body copy and headline work in editorial contexts.
description: Originally commissioned for the redesign of Swedish newspaper Göteborgs-Posten by agency Brandwork, Gothia Sans takes its visual cues from Gothenburg's historical Dutch roots, referencing typefaces by Hendrik van den Keere and Christoffel van Dijck. Söderström gave the sans a smoother, more refined character than its serif counterpart, creating contrast within the Gothia system while maintaining the sharpness and reliability needed for dense editorial work. Three weights — Book, Bold and Black — cover the range from immersive long-form reading to commanding display settings.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Gothia Sans as part of a duo with Gothia Serif, developed under tight deadline conditions for the relaunch of Göteborgs-Posten. The design references Gothenburg's Dutch heritage and the classical types of that era, blending that tradition with a modern Scandinavian sensibility. Designed by Göran Söderström and released in 2019, the family has since been refined and expanded for commercial release.

name: Gothia Serif
classification_notes: Gothia Serif draws directly from 17th-century Dutch typographic tradition, referencing letterforms by Hendrik van den Keere and Christoffel van Dijck, combined with contemporary influences from Gerard Unger, placing it squarely in the Dutch Old Style category.
summary: A Dutch Old Style serif designed for Göteborgs-Posten, drawing on 17th-century Dutch letterforms and inflected with a Scandinavian crispness.
description: Commissioned for the rebrand of Swedish newspaper Göteborgs-Posten by design agency Brandwork, Gothia Serif takes its historical cues from the Dutch city planners who built Gothenburg in the 17th century. Letterforms reference the types of Hendrik van den Keere and Christoffel van Dijck as well as the contemporary work of Gerard Unger, achieving a supple elegance with sharp, precise details. The family is primarily oriented towards display use, with a separate Gothia Serif Text cut available for body copy settings.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Gothia Serif as part of a duo with Gothia Sans, originally created for Göteborgs-Posten's rebrand. The typeface references Gothenburg's historical Dutch origins and draws on classic Dutch types from the 17th century alongside contemporary interpretations, blending that tradition with a modern Scandinavian sensibility. The first versions were produced in under three months to meet the newspaper's launch deadline, with the commercial release refined further for broader use.

name: Gothia Serif Text
classification_notes: Gothia Serif Text draws explicitly on Dutch old-style models from Hendrik van den Keere and Christoffel van Dijck, with a text-optimised adaptation featuring reduced contrast and elevated x-height suited to continuous reading.
summary: A text-optimised spin-off of Gothia Serif by Göran Söderström, adapted from a newspaper typeface with Dutch old-style roots for small-size legibility in books and editorial design.
description: Originally commissioned by Swedish designer Nina Ulmaja for a book project, Gothia Serif Text widens the proportions, reduces stroke contrast and raises the x-height of Gothia Serif to serve continuous reading at small sizes. The parent family was designed for the redesigned Göteborgs-Posten newspaper, drawing on historical Dutch models by Hendrik van den Keere and Christoffel van Dijck alongside contemporary work by Gerard Unger. Two weights, Regular and Book, each with an italic, keep the family compact and purposeful.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Gothia Serif Text as a small text-optimised adaptation of Gothia Serif, created for a book project commissioned by art director Nina Ulmaja. The broader Gothia family was originally designed for Swedish newspaper Göteborgs-Posten and references Gothenburg's historical Dutch connections, combining the Dutch type tradition with a modern Scandinavian sensibility drawn from study of both historical and contemporary classics.

name: Grez
classification_notes: All-caps display typeface constructed from paper-cut letterforms with no drawn sketches or digital construction; the resulting forms are organic and tactile, placing it firmly in the display category with a handcrafted subcategory.
summary: An all-caps display typeface by Ulrika Hellberg, constructed entirely from paper-cut shapes made during a two-week residency in Grez-sur-Loing, France.
description: Each character was cut from black-painted paper using only scissors and a glue stick, with no sketching or ruler allowed, then digitised into a fully functional font. To preserve the handmade variation, each letter ships with four stylistic alternates that randomise as you type. Despite its analogue origins, the family offers broad Latin language support alongside ligatures, mathematical symbols, currency signs and arrows.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Grez as a typeface made entirely from shapes cut out of black paper, created by Ulrika Hellberg during a residency in the historic artists' colony of Grez-sur-Loing near Paris. Working under self-imposed rules — paper, scissors and glue stick only, no sketching, nothing drawn directly in Glyphs — she assembled an A–Z alphabet and digitised it with four randomising stylistic alternates per character, full language support and a wide range of special characters.

name: Inline
classification_notes: A heavy display face with inline counters derived from the stroke weights of companion script Line; the optical sizing system and three widths place it firmly in the display category with a structured, geometric character.
summary: A heavy inline display face by Stefania Malmsten and Göran Söderström, built as a structural counterpart to their script typeface Line, available in three widths and seven optical sizes with a variable font.
description: Conceived as the typographic inverse of Line, a thin graffiti-influenced script, Inline uses counter spaces that correspond directly to Line's varying stroke weights. First deployed as a custom typeface for the SVT documentary Maj 68, it was released commercially in 2020 with 21 styles across three widths, each offering seven optical sizes. A variable font is included, and the Latin character set covers extended European languages.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Inline as their second collaboration with Stefania Malmsten, positioning it as a companion to Line. The counter spaces in each character correspond to the different stroke weights found in Line, making the two families structurally linked. Originally used as a custom face for Swedish public broadcaster SVT, it was later developed into a full release with three widths, seven optical sizes per width, and a variable font.

name: Ivar Display
classification_notes: Ivar is explicitly influenced by Times and the dependable text faces of the mid-1900s, which in turn draw on 16th and 17th century classical designs, placing it firmly in the transitional serif tradition. The high-contrast Display optical size reinforces this classification.
summary: A transitional serif by Göran Söderström in four optical sizes, drawing on Times and the sturdy mid-20th century text faces that preceded it.
description: Designed by Göran Söderström and released in 2017, Ivar takes its cues from Times and the dependable text faces of the mid-1900s, themselves rooted in classical designs from the 16th and 17th centuries. The family spans four optical sizes — Text, Headline, Display and the high-contrast Fine, added in 2020 — each with four weights and matching italics, giving 24 fonts in the core range. A condensed variant and related designs Ivar Nostalgi and Ivar Soft extend the broader Ivar system.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Ivar as beginning as a serif companion to their typeface Siri, though over three years of development the two diverged to the point where only terminal shapes remain shared. Rather than a revival, it draws on the grace and sturdy construction of Times and the trusted text faces of the mid-20th century, with roots in classic 16th and 17th century design. The family comes in three original optical sizes — Text, Headline and Display — each with four weights and italics, with a fourth high-contrast size, Ivar Fine, added in March 2020.

name: Ivar Display Condensed
classification_notes: Described as strongly influenced by Times and the dependable text faces of the mid-1900s, with roots in 16th and 17th century classical designs; the condensed display cut places it firmly in the transitional serif tradition adapted for headline use.
summary: A condensed display serif by Göran Söderström, commissioned for Swedish architecture magazine Arkitektur and rooted in the Times-influenced tradition of mid-century text faces.
description: Originally commissioned by Malmsten Hellberg for the Swedish magazine Arkitektur, this condensed companion to Ivar Display draws on the grace and construction of Times and the dependable text faces of the mid-1900s. Six weights run from Light to Black, giving editorial designers a tight, high-contrast option suited to headlines and feature typography. Part of a broad Ivar family that spans text, headline, fine and soft variants, all sharing only the shapes of their terminals.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Ivar Display Condensed as a condensed version of Ivar Display available in six weights, originally commissioned by Malmsten Hellberg for the Swedish architecture magazine Arkitektur. Ivar began as a serif companion to Siri, and after three years of development the family evolved away from a uniform design, retaining only the terminal shapes across siblings. Rather than a revival, it is strongly influenced by the grace and sturdy construction of Times, with references to reliable mid-century text faces that were themselves rooted in 16th and 17th century classical designs.

name: Ivar Fine
classification_notes: A high-contrast optical size variant within the Ivar family, designed for large headline use with Times-influenced construction and strong modulation between thick and thin strokes.
summary: The highest-contrast optical size in Göran Söderström's Ivar family, built for large-scale headlines where sharpness and elegance take precedence over text legibility.
description: Ivar Fine sits above Ivar Display in contrast, pushing the family's already high modulation further to suit very large point sizes. The design draws on the sturdy mid-twentieth-century text faces in the Times tradition, which themselves reach back to sixteenth and seventeenth-century models. Five weights with matching italics cover the range from restrained Light to assertive Black, all sharing the distinctive terminal shapes that tie the wider Ivar family together.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Ivar Fine as the fourth optical size in the Ivar family, using even higher contrast than Ivar Display for elegance and sharpness at large sizes. The family began as a serif companion for Siri, but after three years of development only the terminal shapes remain shared. Rather than a revival, Ivar is strongly influenced by Times and the dependable text faces of the mid-1900s, which themselves were rooted in classic designs from the 16th and 17th century.

name: Ivar Hand
classification_notes: Based directly on the handwriting of an eight-year-old child, this is an informal script typeface with built-in OpenType randomisation to simulate the natural inconsistency of real handwriting.
summary: A single-weight script typeface drawn from the handwriting of an eight-year-old, with a built-in OpenType randomiser cycling through three alternate letterforms per character.
description: Originally sketched on a Remarkable 2 tablet in 2022 and developed into a font in 2025, this typeface replicates the uneven rhythm of a child's handwriting through contextual alternates. Most letters carry three variants that rotate automatically as you type, avoiding the repetitive look common to single-drawn script fonts. The character set is intentionally limited, so checking available glyphs before licensing is advisable.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Ivar Hand as based on the handwriting of Ivar at eight years old, originally drawn on a Remarkable 2 tablet in 2022 and developed into a font in 2024. The typeface uses a built-in randomiser via the Contextual Alternates OpenType feature, with most letters offering three alternate versions to replicate the subtle inconsistencies of real handwriting. The foundry notes that the character set is incomplete and recommends reviewing available glyphs before licensing.

name: Ivar Headline
classification_notes: Ivar Headline is explicitly influenced by the construction of Times and the dependable text serifs of the mid-20th century, which trace back to 16th and 17th century classical designs, placing it firmly in the transitional serif tradition.
summary: A Times-influenced transitional serif by Göran Söderström, offered as one of four optical sizes within a broad editorial family.
description: Designed by Göran Söderström and released in 2017, Ivar draws on the construction and grace of Times without functioning as a strict revival, instead referencing the dependable text faces of the mid-20th century and their classical antecedents. The Headline optical size sits between the Text and Display cuts, offering four weights from Regular to Bold with matching italics. The wider family extends to Ivar Fine, a high-contrast variant added in 2020, and further into Display Condensed, Nostalgi and Soft.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Ivar as beginning as a serif companion for their typeface Siri, evolving over three years into an independent design that shares only terminal shapes with its sibling. Strongly influenced by Times rather than reviving it, Ivar spans three original optical sizes — Text, Headline and Display — each with four weights and matching italics, with a fourth high-contrast optical size, Ivar Fine, added in March 2020.

name: Ivar Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced serif explicitly described as such by the foundry, derived from the Ivar serif family with optical sizing balanced between text and display contrast.
summary: A monospaced serif by Göran Söderström, spun off from the Ivar family with contrast pitched between its text and headline optical sizes.
description: Derived from Göran Söderström's Ivar typeface, this monospaced variant was originally designed for GRÖN and arrives in five weights from Light to Black, with a variable font included in the Complete package. The optical sizing sits deliberately between Ivar Text and Ivar Headline, giving it enough contrast to feel elegant while remaining workable at body text sizes. Its extended Latin character set covers a wide range of European languages, and the inclusion of fractions, super- and subscripts, and currency symbols makes it well suited to technical and editorial work alike.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Ivar Mono as a monospaced version of Ivar in five styles, designed by Göran Söderström and released in 2023. Its optical size is calibrated between Ivar Text and Ivar Headline so it functions for body text while retaining higher contrast for elegance. The typeface was originally created for GRÖN, and a variable font is included in the Complete package.

name: Ivar Nostalgi
classification_notes: Ivar Nostalgi is a high-contrast serif with extreme stroke contrast and tight letterspacing, explicitly referencing 1970s phototypesetting-era display faces like Times Modern and ITC Grouch. Its stencil cut and optical variants place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single bold-weight, high-contrast display serif in four optical styles including a stencil cut, evoking the exuberant phototypesetting aesthetics of the 1970s.
description: Designed by Göran Söderström and released in 2018, it draws on faces like Times Modern, Caslon Graphique and ITC Grouch to recreate the tight spacing and extreme contrast of 1970s phototypesetting-era display type. Four optical styles progress from tight hairlines to a stencil version where those hairlines disappear entirely, giving the family an unusual formal range within a single weight. The result reads as familiar without being derivative, producing the déjà-vu effect its name promises.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Ivar Nostalgi as a special cut of Ivar tailored for compact, high-impact headlines, inspired by the explosion of expressive display type that phototypesetting made possible from the 1950s onward. The face flirts with almost-forgotten 1970s references including Times Modern, Caslon Graphique, Trooper Roman, Hawthorn and ITC Grouch, and is offered in one bold weight across four optical styles, with hairlines growing progressively finer until they vanish in the stencil version.

name: Ivar Script
classification_notes: A connected, flowing script derived from the italics of the Ivar serif family, blending formal italic structure with the fluidity of handwritten script. Described explicitly as a script with optical sizes and contextual alternates for connected letterforms.
summary: A connected script grown from the italic of the Ivar typeface, originally commissioned for Sotheby's Magazine and offered in three optical sizes with extensive OpenType control over joining behaviour.
description: Designed by Göran Söderström and released in 2025, Ivar Script extends the Ivar italic into connected script territory, keeping the elegance of a formal serif italic while introducing the flow of a handwritten hand. Three optical sizes — Large, Medium and Small — are sold as a single package, each tuned for different contexts. Contextual Alternates handle connections automatically, while three Stylistic Sets give typographers manual control over capital-to-lowercase joins, alternate capitals and trailing connecting tails.
foundry_blurb: Ivar Script is described by Letters from Sweden as a connected extension of the Ivar italic, mixing the elegance of a serif italic with the fluidity of a script. Originally commissioned in 2024 for Sotheby's Magazine by Magnus Berger and Henrik Zachrisson, it comes in three optical sizes powered by Contextual Alternates, with additional Stylistic Sets for capital connections, alternate capitals and retained connecting tails at word endings. Production was handled by Léo Guibert.

name: Ivar Soft
classification_notes: Ivar is explicitly influenced by Times and mid-20th century text faces rooted in 16th–17th century classics, placing it firmly in the transitional serif tradition. The Soft cut adds rounded terminals for warmth while retaining the underlying construction.
summary: A rounded cut of Ivar Headline, originally commissioned by Pentagram, softening the transitional serif construction with rounded terminals for a friendlier tone.
description: Ivar Soft takes the forms of Ivar Headline and rounds the terminals, lending the transitional serif warmth without sacrificing its structural rigour. The family was designed by Göran Söderström with Greek characters by Panos Haratzopoulos, and covers Latin and Greek scripts across four weights with matching italics. Originally a bespoke commission from Pentagram, the cut is now available for retail alongside the broader Ivar family, which spans Text, Headline, Display and Fine optical sizes.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Ivar Soft as a custom cut of Ivar Headline that has been rounded for warmth and friendliness, originally commissioned by Pentagram for a client and now available for retail. Ivar itself was conceived as a serif companion for Siri, taking influence from the grace and sturdy construction of Times and the dependable text faces of the mid-1900s, which in turn were rooted in classic designs from the 16th and 17th century.

name: Ivar Text
classification_notes: Ivar is described as strongly influenced by Times and the dependable text faces of the mid-1900s, themselves rooted in 16th and 17th century classics — placing it firmly in the transitional serif tradition with a contemporary optical-size structure.
summary: A Times-influenced text serif by Göran Söderström, released across four optical sizes with four weights and matching italics in each.
description: Drawn over three years as a companion to Letters from Sweden's Siri, Ivar ultimately went its own way, retaining only shared terminal shapes. The design draws on mid-20th-century text faces rooted in 16th and 17th century models, with Times as a key reference point for its sturdy construction and grace. The family spans Text, Headline, Display and the high-contrast Fine optical sizes, each offering four weights with italics, totalling 24 fonts across the core three and more with Fine.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Ivar as beginning life as a serif companion for Siri, evolving over three years into a design that shares only terminal shapes with its sibling. Rather than a revival, Ivar takes strong influence from the grace and sturdy construction of Times, referencing dependable mid-20th-century text faces whose own roots lie in classical 16th and 17th century designs. In March 2020 a fourth optical size, Ivar Fine, was added as a high-contrast variant. Related families include Ivar Display Condensed, Ivar Nostalgi and Ivar Soft.

name: Kumla
classification_notes: Kumla is an all-caps geometric display face drawn from vernacular architectural lettering of the 1920s; its monoline construction and capital-only origins place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight display face drawn from the 1920s architectural lettering on a shoe factory in Kumla, Sweden.
description: Göran Söderström revived the capital letterforms spelled out on the facade of Kumla Skofabrik, a shoe factory dating from 1912, and released the result in 2013. The face is all-caps in origin, carrying the monoline geometry of early twentieth-century commercial signage, and covers both Latin Extended and Cyrillic scripts. Its character set is broad enough for multilingual work despite having just the one weight.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Kumla as inspired by the capital letters on the front of Kumla Skofabrik, a shoe factory built in 1912 in the Swedish town of Kumla, once the epicentre of Swedish shoe manufacturing. The original letters date from around 1920, and while no one knows who drew them, the typeface is dedicated to that unknown craftsman. Designed by Göran Söderström and released in 2013.

name: Lab Antiqua
classification_notes: A high-contrast serif with large flat serifs and refined hairlines, drawing explicitly on Century Expanded, Bodoni and De Vinne as formal precursors, placing it in the classical high-contrast tradition with contemporary optical sizing.
summary: Originally developed as a bespoke typeface for Stockholm Design Lab's 20th anniversary, growing from the agency's own logotype into a full editorial and identity family with three optical sizes and a variable font.
description: Rooted in the SDL logotype designed in 2008, the family takes its cues from the wordmark's flat serifs, pronounced contrast and the distinctive flat-topped S, drawing further inspiration from Century Expanded, Bodoni and De Vinne. Two widths and three optical sizes ensure hairlines and serifs hold at any size, while a variable font allows continuous tuning across both axes. A stylistic set removes capital overshoots to prevent collisions in tightly stacked all-caps settings, making it particularly suited to covers, posters and fashion packaging.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Lab Antiqua as a three-year collaboration with Stockholm Design Lab to mark the agency's 20th anniversary in 2018, turning their existing wordmark into a premium typeface family. The design traces its distinctive traits — large flat serifs, refined high contrast and a flat-topped S — back to the 2008 SDL logotype, with nods to Morris Fuller Benton's Century Expanded, Bodoni and De Vinne by Gustav F. Schroeder. The family covers Latin and Cyrillic scripts, comes in two widths across three optical sizes, and includes a variable font with an optional stylistic set for all-caps titling.

name: Lab Grotesque
designer: Stockholm Design Lab, Göran Söderström, Panos Haratzopoulos
classification_notes: Explicitly positioned against Helvetica's rigidity in favour of earlier turn-of-the-century grotesks and gothics; curved strokes straightening to terminals is a classic grotesque structural trait.
summary: A six-weight grotesque designed with Stockholm Design Lab, drawing on turn-of-the-century grotesks rather than the neutrality of Helvetica.
description: Designed by Göran Söderström with Stockholm Design Lab and released in 2015, it is built around curved strokes that straighten towards the terminals, a detail that gives it quiet character without ornament. Stylistic sets allow switching between square and rounded dots, and the family covers Latin (including Vietnamese), Cyrillic and Greek with Cyrillic consultation from Maria Doreuli. A companion monospaced family, Lab Grotesque Mono, extends the system for code or tabular work.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Lab Grotesque as conceptually built on the idea of curved strokes straightening out towards the terminals, looking past the repression of Helvetica to the idiosyncrasies of earlier grotesks and gothics from the turn of the century. Originally designed with and for Stockholm Design Lab, it comes in six weights with italics and includes Latin (with Vietnamese), Greek and Cyrillic, and is accompanied by Lab Grotesque Mono.

name: Lab Grotesque Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced companion to the Lab Grotesque family, it retains grotesque proportions while embracing the visual artefacts of fixed-width spacing rather than concealing them.
summary: A two-weight monospaced companion to Lab Grotesque that deliberately accentuates the quirks of fixed-width spacing rather than smoothing them away.
description: Designed by Stockholm Design Lab and Göran Söderström and released in 2017, this family extends Lab Grotesque into monospaced territory with Regular and Bold weights. The design philosophy treats the visual distortions of forcing proportional letters into fixed widths as a feature rather than a flaw. Script coverage spans Latin with Vietnamese support, Cyrillic and Greek.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Lab Grotesque Mono as a monospaced version of Lab Grotesque available in Regular and Bold. Rather than concealing the consequences of adapting proportional letters to a fixed grid, the designers chose to emphasise the effect. The family supports Latin including Vietnamese, Greek and Cyrillic.

name: Line
classification_notes: Line is a super-thin display face inspired by handwriting, graffiti tags and scribble, with modular embellishments and alternate letters that give it a distinctly gestural, decorative character placing it firmly in the display script category.
summary: A super-thin display typeface designed for Swedish fashion magazine Rodeo, combining gestural handwriting influences with modular embellishments built from kerning pairs.
description: Originally commissioned for the Swedish fashion and culture magazine Rodeo in 2012, Line was designed by Stefania Malmsten and Göran Söderström and released commercially in 2013. It comes in five ultra-thin styles intended for use individually or via a formula that keeps stroke weight consistent across point sizes. The family includes alternate letters and a large set of modular decorative elements that attach to characters through kerning pairs, drawing inspiration from beauty, handwriting, graffiti tags and scribble.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Line as a typeface originally created for Swedish fashion and culture magazine Rodeo in 2012, designed with Stefania Malmsten and released commercially in 2013. It offers five super-thin styles and a modular system of embellishments that attach to letters via kerning pairs, inspired by beauty, handwriting, graffiti tags and scribble.

name: Markelius Sans
classification_notes: Derived from hand-drawn Helvetica interpretations found in a 1960s Functionalist building, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition with the irregular warmth of vernacular signage lettering.
summary: A grotesque drawn from hand-lettered Helvetica approximations found in the 1960s signage archives of Sven Markelius's Folkets Hus in Stockholm.
description: Digitised directly from original signboards at a landmark Stockholm Functionalist building, each letterform carries the slight irregularity of hand-drawn craft that no purely geometric construction would produce. Four weights with italics span from regular to black, and a variable version is included alongside a unicase stylistic set and four arrow sets aligned to both lowercase and capital heights. Commissioned by Figur for the 2025 reopening of Folkets Hus, the typeface carries a specific institutional and architectural history rather than the neutrality its grotesque lineage might suggest.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Markelius Sans as based on hand-drawn interpretations of Helvetica discovered in the archives of Folkets Hus at Norra Bantorget, a Functionalist building designed by architect Sven Markelius. The letters were used for signage at the building's 1960 inauguration and again in 1991, and were digitised directly from that source material, with missing characters drawn to follow the same underlying logic. They position it as an antidote to contemporary typographic perfectionism, released in 2026 in four weights with italics and a variable version.

name: Mikro
classification_notes: Mikro is explicitly described as a geometric sans, positioned as a companion to the grotesque Trim and drawing on Futura's influence in Swedish vernacular. The design steps outside a strict pixel grid, giving it a more free geometric character.
summary: A geometric sans by Ramiro Oblitas and Göran Söderström, commissioned by Design Sweden and rooted in Futura's long presence in Swedish graphic culture.
description: Designed as a counterpart to Letters from Sweden's grotesque Trim, Mikro draws on the geometric tradition associated with Futura, which the foundry describe as fundamental to Swedish visual identity. Originally commissioned by Design Sweden to represent both established and contemporary styles, it covers Latin and Cyrillic scripts across three weights with matching italics. A companion display subfamily, Mikro Super, was subsequently released for tight headline use.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Mikro as a diverging path from their grotesque Trim, taking on the geometric sans in its place. Futura, they note, has always been fundamental in Swedish vernacular graphic culture, playing into a collective self-image. The typeface was commissioned by Design Sweden, who wanted something representative of both established and contemporary styles, prompting the studio to revisit that parallel design direction.

name: Mikro Super
classification_notes: A bold display-weight extension of the Mikro family, designed specifically for tight headlines at large sizes. Its single-weight Black and companion Bold and Medium cuts place it firmly in the display category rather than a workhorse text family.
summary: A heavy display companion to Mikro by Göran Söderström, built for tight headlines with a unicase stylistic set included.
description: Released in 2021, this is the display-weight offshoot of Göran Söderström's Mikro, offered in three weights from Medium to Black and intended specifically for large, close-set type. A unicase stylistic set adds a secondary register for more expressive headline work. Latin and Cyrillic script coverage makes it viable across a broad range of European and Slavic language contexts.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Mikro Super as a bolder display version of Mikro, specially designed for tight headlines. The family includes a Unicase stylistic set and covers Latin and Cyrillic scripts across three weights.

name: Rund Display
classification_notes: Geometric sans with circular, square and triangular stroke rhythms, citing Futura, Avenir and Neuzeit Grotesk as references. The Display cut features exaggerated concave terminal shapes that further distinguish it from conventional geometric sans archetypes.
summary: A geometric sans by Göran Söderström with subtly concave stroke terminals that add warmth and crispness, particularly at large sizes.
description: Rund Display draws on the geometric sans tradition of Futura, Avenir and Neuzeit Grotesk while introducing concave terminal shapes reminiscent of Optima, referencing broad-nib pen forms. Eight display weights ship with tight spacing and exaggerated terminals designed to become visible at large sizes without disrupting readability at text sizes in its companion Text cut. A variable font is available on request.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Rund as a geometric sans built on friendly, functional foundations with cues from Neuzeit Grotesk, Futura and Avenir. Its distinguishing feature is subtly concave terminal shapes, similar to those in Optima, which reference the broad-nib pen and emphasise the typeface's roundness. The family comprises eight Text weights with italics and a Display version in eight weights with exaggerated terminals and tight spacing, plus an optional variable font.

name: Rund Text
classification_notes: A geometric sans serif with round forms drawing on Futura, Avenir and Neuzeit Grotesk lineage. The subtly concave stroke terminals distinguish it from typical geometric genre entries.
summary: A geometric sans by Göran Söderström that distinguishes itself through subtly concave stroke terminals, echoing broad-nib pen forms and lending warmth to an otherwise classical geometric structure.
description: Released in 2021, Rund draws on the tradition of Futura, Avenir and Neuzeit Grotesk while adding a specific detail: curved strokes end in slightly concave terminals, as though cut by a large circle, a device that references Optima and broad-nib penmanship. This quality becomes visible at display sizes, adding personality without compromising the reliability expected of the genre at text sizes. The family spans eight Text weights with italics and a separate Display subfamily with exaggerated terminals and tighter spacing, with a variable font available on request.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Rund as a fresh approach to the geometric sans, built on friendly and functional foundations that take cues from Neuzeit Grotesk, Futura and Avenir. They highlight its concave stroke terminals as the distinguishing feature, similar to those in Optima, which they say refer to broad-nib pen forms and add warmth and crispness. The family comprises eight Text weights with italics and a Display version in eight weights with exaggerated terminals and tight spacing.

name: Scoop
classification_notes: Scoop draws directly from the grotesque tradition, with a balanced structure informed by dependable newspaper grotesques and stylistic alternates for key characters typical of the genre.
summary: A grotesque designed by Göran Söderström as the custom typeface DN Sans for Swedish daily newspaper Dagens Nyheter, now available commercially.
description: Originally commissioned as DN Sans for Dagens Nyheter, one of Sweden's oldest daily newspapers, the family launched in 2023 across print, web and app. Six weights with matching italics and a variable font are included, and five stylistic sets allow substitution of characters such as a, g, t, 6 and 9, along with a toggle between circular and square punctuation. A condensed variant is in production.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Scoop as drawing inspiration from dependable and versatile grotesque typefaces, striking a balance between uniqueness and simplicity. Originally designed as DN Sans for Dagens Nyheter, it was launched in 2023 for the newspaper's printed edition, website and mobile app, and is now available as a commercial retail typeface.

name: Siri
classification_notes: Siri has a large x-height, slightly condensed letterforms and a strong identity consistent with humanist sans-serif design principles, drawing on Scandinavian typographic sensibility.
summary: A slightly condensed humanist sans by Göran Söderström with 16 styles, a large x-height and broad Latin coverage, released in 2011.
description: Designed by Göran Söderström and predating Apple's voice assistant by some years, Siri is a workhorse sans with a large x-height and slightly condensed proportions suited to editorial and identity work. The 16-style family spans Thin to Bold with italics and includes a comprehensive extended Latin character set covering most European languages. OpenType features add practical utility across a range of typographic applications.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Siri as a labour of love for original, bold, practical and beautiful letterforms. The name is a feminine Nordic word meaning beauty and strength, qualities they believe are reflected in the typeface. Released in 2011, before Apple's voice assistant of the same name, it offers 16 styles, a large x-height, slightly condensed letterforms and useful OpenType features.

name: Sväng
classification_notes: A stencil display face built from dynamic geometric forms with deliberate counters removed, placing it firmly in the display category with a geometric stencil subcategory, akin to Novarese's Stop and Motter Tektura.
summary: A geometric stencil display face originally drawn by Claes Nordenstam in 1976 and revived by Letters from Sweden in 2019, with surprising letterforms built by strategically removing parts of the glyphs.
description: Originally titled Quickstep, the face was drawn in 1976 by Claes Nordenstam under the influence of his tutor Erik Lindegren and sat undiscovered on paper for over four decades before Letters from Sweden digitised it. The design shares conceptual territory with Aldo Novarese's Stop and Othmar Motter's Motter Tektura, but its range of three weights from Thin to Bold and broader glyph coverage make it more adaptable than its peers. The stencil geometry carries an unmistakable 1970s rhythm that works across posters, editorial headings and identity work that calls for strong visual character.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe finding Sväng as discovering hidden treasure, noting that Nordenstam's original drawings had never been digitised. They position it as one of the most original display faces of the 1970s, exploring stencil geometry in the spirit of Stop and Motter Tektura but with greater versatility, and released it in 2019 after Nordenstam, now a full-time painter and jazz musician, collaborated with Göran Söderström to bring it to life.

name: Swedish Gothic
classification_notes: A European grotesque sans with narrow letterforms, open apertures and a large x-height, drawing on Otl Aicher's typefaces and American Gothic traditions, toggled via Stylistic Sets.
summary: A narrow grotesque by Göran Söderström that navigates between Aicher's European rationalism and American Gothic tradition via Stylistic Sets.
description: Designed by Göran Söderström and first used in Malmsten Hellberg's redesign of Tidskriften Arkitektur, Swedish Gothic draws on Otl Aicher's work alongside various European and American grotesque references before settling into its own voice. Narrow letterforms, open apertures and a large x-height make it well-suited to demanding editorial conditions. Stylistic Sets let users shift between the European and American flavours, effectively giving two distinct personalities within a single family.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Swedish Gothic as the epitome of an investigative attitude: finding something new in the old, or something old in the new. Sparked by a long-held admiration for Otl Aicher's typefaces, it gathered influences from iconic European sans serifs and American Gothics during development before finding its own voice. Stylistic Sets allow users to toggle between the two flavours with a simple click.

name: Tid
designer: Göran Söderström, Stefania Malmsten, Laurette Colmard
classification_notes: Tid is a text and display serif with horizontally and vertically cut serifs, harmonious cap-to-lowercase balance, and mirrored italics — characteristics consistent with a refined transitional or classical serif tradition.
summary: A text and display serif by Göran Söderström and Stefania Malmsten, first cut for a Laurie Anderson exhibition at Moderna Museet in Stockholm in 2023.
description: Designed by Göran Söderström and Stefania Malmsten as an interpretation of what they call The Best Typeface in the World, Tid is available in ten text styles and four display cuts. Serifs and tails are cut consistently either horizontally or vertically, weights are designed to occupy similar widths, and the italics closely mirror the romans. Extensive Latin character support and careful optical sizing make it suited to both editorial and institutional use.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Tid as their interpretation of The Best Typeface in the World — a typeface and an idea that Söderström and Malmsten have been preoccupied with throughout their careers. First shown during Moderna Museet's Laurie Anderson exhibition in spring 2023, the 2024 release offers ten text styles and four display cuts with broad language support and consistent horizontal and vertical serif treatment.

name: Trim
classification_notes: Trim is a geometric sans-serif distinguished by its trimmed diagonal and curved stems, a concept derived from the work of Danish designer Knud V. Engelhardt. The systematic cutting of terminals across all letterforms gives it a distinctive angular, constructed quality.
summary: A geometric sans by Göran Söderström that applies systematic stem-trimming across every possible letterform, inspired by the work of Danish designer Knud V. Engelhardt.
description: Released in 2012 by Letters from Sweden, Trim extends a design principle drawn from Engelhardt's trimmed diagonal stems to cover all letters in the family, not just diagonals. The result is a large, wide-ranging family from very light to black weights, with coverage across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek scripts. Companion families Trim Poster and Trim Mono extend its range for display and monospaced applications.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Trim as taking the concept of trimming letterforms to a whole new level, applying it not only to diagonals but to all possible letters. Designed by Göran Söderström with Greek support by Panos Haratzopoulos and Cyrillic consultation from Maria Doreuli, the family covers Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, with Arabic available on request.

name: Trim Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with a distinctive geometric character derived from systematically trimming stems and diagonals, placing it firmly in the geometric mono category.
summary: A monospaced typeface by Göran Söderström that extends the stem-trimming technique associated with Knud V. Engelhardt's letterforms to every possible character.
description: Where typefaces like Skilt Gothic selectively trim diagonal stems as a stylistic device, Trim Mono applies the same principle indiscriminately across the entire character set, producing something noticeably eccentric. The family spans at least three weights from Light to Bold. Latin script coverage is broad, including extended characters for Nordic and Central European languages.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Trim Mono as inspired by Danish architect and designer Knud V. Engelhardt, whose trimmed diagonals have already influenced modern typefaces including Skilt Gothic. Rather than repeat that approach, designer Göran Söderström pushed the concept further by trimming not just diagonals but all possible letterforms, yielding a family that ranges from very light to very heavy weights.

name: Trim Poster
classification_notes: An extremely narrow display typeface designed specifically for compact headline setting with diacritics in mind, spanning multiple extreme width variants from Skinny to Fat. Its primary function is display and poster use rather than text setting.
summary: A slender display family by Göran Söderström engineered for tight headline setting with diacritics, spanning eight widths from Skinny to Fat.
description: Designed specifically to solve the problem of diacritical marks crashing into neighbouring lines at tight leading, Trim Poster offers two stylistic sets for diacritic variants so Scandinavian characters like Å, Ä and Ö can be set without manual adjustment. Eight widths run from Skinny to Fat with a variable version included in the complete family licence. Updated in 2016 to add lowercase glyphs, making it workable across a broader range of headline applications.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Trim Poster as a member of the Trim family designed with one purpose: compact headlines without crashing. They note that in many languages, diacritical marks above and below letters cause problems at tight line-heights, and that Swedish tabloids routinely make custom adjustments to handle Å, Ä and Ö. Trim Poster is their answer, offering eight cuts from Skinny to Fat and stylistic sets for two diacritic styles, with a variable version included in the complete family licence.

name: Trim Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil display typeface built around the concept of trimming stems and diagonals, derived from the geometric letterform approach of Danish designer Knud V. Engelhardt. Its single-weight, uppercase-focused construction places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A stencil display face by Göran Söderström that takes Knud V. Engelhardt's trimmed-stem letterform concept and applies it across every possible character.
description: Inspired by Danish architect and designer Knud V. Engelhardt, whose trimmed diagonal stems had already influenced typefaces such as Skilt Gothic, Söderström extended the idea further by applying the trimming treatment to all letters, not only diagonals. The result is a single ExtraBold stencil weight with broad Latin character support including extended diacritics and special characters. Part of the wider Trim family, which spans a range of weights from heavy to narrow.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Trim Stencil as part of a larger Trim family, noting its roots in Knud V. Engelhardt's characteristic trimmed letterforms. Where those forms had previously influenced faces like Skilt Gothic in a measured way, Trim Stencil takes the concept further by trimming all possible letters, not just the diagonals.

name: Voir
classification_notes: Built from a geometric circle and a simple stroke combined in unusual proportions, Voir is an ultra-thin display typeface with strong optical personality and an extensive alternate system, placing it firmly in the geometric display category.
summary: An ultra-thin geometric display typeface built from just two shapes, a circle and a stroke, with an unusually large set of alternates for art direction work.
description: Designed by Swedish art director Pompe Hedengren and type designer Göran Söderström, Voir derives all its glyphs from a geometric circle and a simple stroke combined at unusual angles and proportions. Seven optical weights run from the extreme end of thin, and sixteen stylistic sets give art directors a wide palette of alternates rarely matched at this scale. The result sits somewhere between rigorous geometry and playful personality, making it well suited to editorial and poster contexts where precision and wit can coexist.
foundry_blurb: Letters from Sweden describe Voir as an ultra-thin typeface in seven optical weights by multi-awarded Swedish art director Pompe Hedengren and Göran Söderström. Starting from two basic shapes, a geometric circle and a simple stroke, the design combines them in unusual proportions and angles to construct the full glyph set. They describe it as mixing extreme elegance with a sense of humour, and note it ships with more alternates than you would probably ever need.
